text: Everyone assumes that Luffy has fallen victim to the common shonen trope of “main character has secretly been destined to win the whole time due to bloodline”. I do not believe Oda would do that to us. Let’s recap- Luffy ate the Gum Gum fruit by happenstance. He was never meant to get ahold of it. He has worked harder than anyone else we’ve seen in universe specifically to become the pirate king and achieve his as of yet unknown true dream. He had accepted his imminent death in Loguetown, and beyond that accepted the fact he may die at every turn. He is willing to put his aspirations aside at a moments notice to fight injustice or save his friends, or anyone who has just given him a sandwich recently. He was not chosen to be the incarnation of Nika- he earned it and was recognized as having earned it by the fruit. Anyone familiar with the Zelda series knows that of all of the incarnations of Link, the one from Wind Waker was not the incarnation chosen by the gods like most others across the series. He was unique in that he was a simple boy who’s sister got kidnapped, set out to save her, and after conquering the tower of the gods was recognized as being worthy to take up the mantle of hero. Likewise, the true form of the gum gum fruit, and indeed Nika/Joyboy/ whatever, did not choose Luffy. Luffy chose his own fate and was recognized as being worthy of that mantle.
label: analysis

text: Rocks is from God Valley and learned all about the history of the world from the Davy clan people and artifacts there. How else would he have known about Imu in Mary Geoise? I don't think Imu's motivations are simply "because evil". I think there are all reasonable explanations for how things have ended up this way and some may even seem reasonable. Rocks didn't kill Imu in Marie Geoise because he didn't know how to. He was trying to work out what Imu's weak spot is to usurp them instead. He took 5 kings hostage on the assumption that Imu would care and was frustrated when Imu said they didn't give a fuck about the kings, because they really don't. Rocks then goes on to tell Harald that children are a parent's weakness, meaning it's likely that Imu has children and this will be exploited against them (probably by Blackbeard).
label: analysis

text: This may just be my English literature class influencing me, but I've been thinking about some possible symbolism for the Straw Hat. I think that it represents desire and ambition to become a legendary pirate/Pirate King/find One Piece, and also inherited will. Now some reasons why I think so; Gold Roger started with the hat, we see him playing with it when he asks Rayleigh to join his crew and "turn the world upside down." Then when he's finished, he passes it on to the next generation, Shanks, an aspiring pirate. For whatever reason Shanks sees something special in Luffy and realizes that he must be the one. So he hands it to Luffy, and makes him promise that he will be a great pirate one day. Luffy hasn't left the hat since, and he has stood by his desire to be Pirate King. More smaller details; Luffy gives his hat to Nami and pauses his journey to become Pirate King in order to defeat Arlong as well as protect his nakama. During the timeskip he takes off his hat to, again, put his dream on standby in order to train and get stronger so that he can survive in the New World. The hat being his "treasure" could also be referencing the symbolism in that it represents his ambition to find One Piece.
label: analysis

text: The idea of adult messing things up for the new generation is not a new one, especially in Anime. If you ever play Persona 5, or watch any arc of Naruto (the last gigantic arc they basically had to fight with zombies of the adults that ruined their lives in the past, some going back several generations), Japanese theme of adults messing up the world for kids is not new. I believe this is the only arc of One Piece that has this message though. This is reinforced when Luffy said a grandpa's sin is not the grandchild's fault (referencing both Garp, Don Chinjao, and Rebecca's grandfather).

text: Dressrosa was a country shrouded in metaphorical darkness before Luffy made the sun shine again.The sun is what rises after the dawn, and Luffy is considered the dawn of the world. We saw the sunflower during Nami's flashback when Bellemere gave her a t shirt that had a lion sunflower on it (ch. 77), likely foreshadowing that Luffy, the dawn of the world, the bringer of the sun, would free her and her entire village at some time in the future. The lion in Christian theology symbolizes Christ, savior of humankind and bringer of light. So I think the sunflower was there to in part reinforce Luffy's Christ like figure in the context of Dressrosa who needed liberation from a false king.
label: analysis

text: their boat being the Sunny is a brilliant piece of symbolism in itself. Both carrying the sun that "brings the dawn of the world", and further establishing Luffy as a Messianic leader figure. The lion commonly means royalty and power (king of the pirates), and is a common way of reffering to God or Christ.
label: analysis

text: the Donquixote family is comprised entirely of adults barring Doflamingo. The family has the visage of children but their actions and behavior indicates otherwise (Senor Pink's apparel being indicative of his grief, Baby 5's behavior borne from abandonment, the Executives' childish behavior to belie their deviousness and cruelty, etc). I view the Donquixote Family as a group of enablers who have adopted a child and propped him onto a pedestal which has given said child a false sense of importance. I point you to the scene where the Executives find Doflamingo and bow before him as he stands atop a pile of rubble (the proverbial pedestal). This can also be seen in the relationship between Giolla and Dellinger, as the adult took on this child and molded them into becoming a very heinous creature.

text: Zeff described Luffy as a spear once, and he behaved like a spear in almost every major arc since Foxy. In most arc, Luffy is spent running towards something with the other straw hats trying to clear his path and the enemies keep getting in the way. This is how it was in Impel Down, Marineford, a bit of Punk Hazard, and Dressrosa. In Water 7, even though Rocket Man was stopped at the entrance, Luffy kept going (and Rocket Man did kept going) not listening to anyone. Zoro literally had to hold Luffy back as he was trying to run after Robin... (he was actually obsessed with Rob Lucci the pigeon guy from the start). This is what he does, head straight for the boss. He did so even in Amazon Lilly, reasoning that the boss must live in the highest building and headed straight for it. As the matter of fact, Luffy punched Vivi for not letting him go straight to beat up Crocodile, and also fought with Jinbei a bit on Fishman Island because Jinbei asked him not to head straight for Hody. So it makes sense that the straw hat would ride on Rocket Man into battle. Luffy is him. I feel kinda bad after reading in the wiki that it was just left in the ruins of Enies Lobby. It should have been put in a Water 7 museum or something.
label: analysis

text: the huge tsunami that the straw hat had to literally go through is symbolic of having to weathered the storm together and pass through the giant wave of obstacle in front of them. May be there's a Japanese saying that better describe it. But every language has a saying about going through some type of wave I think.There's not much more to analyze about it, except the fact that it was mentioned over and over again that "this is the worst Aqua Laguna in several years." This could just mean that the enemy is the biggest straw hat has ever battled. One thing that a tsunami does that most people are unaware before it became common knowledge after the 2004 tsunami (and later Japan tsunami) is that before it hits, the sea would recede first. This was in the manga when Luffy and Zoro were stuck in between buildings after being thrown by Lucci. The wave receding revealed broken city underneath Water 7. It might be a bit of a stretch, but it outline the structure of the arc quite well. First, the water receded to reveal the ugly side of everyone, especially Luffy Usopp and Robin. Luffy for being a bad leader by not accepting the truth about Merry at first, couldn't calm and keep Usopp in line, and Robin's past, which he didn't care about, came back to bite everyone in the ass. Then of course, the wave hit representing ruler of the sea, World Government.
label: analysis

text: While this goes beyond Water 7, Franky's several Battle Franky creations were made, including part of himself, on pile of discarded ships in the Scrap island. By the way, in case it wasn't obvious, and I was surprised it wasn't in the wiki, the name Franky comes from Frankenstein. His metal parts are from "corpses" of discarded ship. Most straw hat members were orphaned or abandoned in the flashback. The only members of straw hat that weren't discarded in some way was.... Merry. Merry was well designed and was given as a gift to straw hat. But Merry did make a trip to the scrap island. It was there that Iceberg fixed it for one last ride out.
label: analysis

text: Unlike Goku, Naruto, or even Zoro, we never see Luffy "train hard" until we get Ace flashback and he spent time with Rayleigh learning Haki. But everytime Luffy gets a new move, it often looks similar to what he had "seen". He imitates Sanji and even himself frozen for visual gag. But he imitates fighting style also. He is almost like Taskmaster.His gear second imitated the steam engine that he saw, either Puffing Tom or Rocket Man. But his other moves he gained this arc imitated CP9's own moves, most noticably Soru and Geppo. After he sees something being done, Luffy just copies it. Luffy even thank Blueno for being there. He did this several times in later arc. His Gear 3rd probably DEFINITELY imitated the moves of the giants he encountered which he always remember since he noticed that Big Mom has similar move. The name of his moves are very telling with the way they are named All of his Gear 2nd moves have the word Jet in them (jet steam, not jet plane) All of his Gear 3rd moves have Giant in them... yes because his limbs are enlarged, but Giant Axe? That's a clear reference to Brogy, one of the giant he met on Little Garden.AFTER the Time Skip. All of his moves are named after animals. Gear Third's Elephant, Grizzly. and Gear Fourth's Serpent, Lion, and of course Monkey King, may referenced the animals he encountered during his training?
label: analysis

text: What are the significance of people wearing masks at the beginning of Water 7. The whole mask thing was just abandoned when the CP9 revealed themselves and everybody was focused on Aqua Laguna. Did the masks meant anything at all? Franky was introduced wearing it and we never seen him does it again. But one straw hat made use of a mask. Usopp. Symbolically, mask is about "the face you choose". We don't really choose what we look like, we only dress it up sometime. But when we put on the mask, we become someone who we choose to be, OR, who we truly are. You know, the whole Bruce Wayne is not pretending to be Batman, but it's Batman that sometimes pretend to be Bruce Wayne, kinda thing.When CP9 put on the masks, that's who they really are, evil scary mfs. When they took off the masks, it revealed the face that we knew from earlier, but they are their trueselves then.

text: Who is Sogeking? Sogeking is a straw hat member, who can pretty much be called "the NOT USOPP". Sogeking is useful, because Usopp was useless. The reason why he broke away from Straw Hat wasn't really because of Merry, but because he felt useless. Merry was his responsibility and he couldn't fix it. That's why he felt so guilty with the whole money and Franky thing, and lashed out at Luffy when Luffy said to abandon Merry. To Usopp, HE WAS MERRY. And if they abandoned Merry for not being useful anymore, he feels that he will get abandoned some day too. Same thing Robin feared will happen if the government kept going after them because of her.
label: analysis

text: Rob Lucci really hates Spandam. In fact, he openly admits that he admired Luffy as a leader. He also lead a revenge on Franky's family for Usopp, something that, though satisfying, resulted in more trouble for Straw hat. text: I just wanted to share what I've come across and realized recently that blew my mind. We all know that Oda loves puns and double-triple-quad-infinite entendras that he weaves in to names of characters, locations, and the like. Furthermore, numbers having meaning is a huge thing in eastern societies, especially in Japan. And, if you watch the video I mentioned, you'll see concrete proof that Oda loves these perfect round numbers and connecting the dots using them. I think I may have discovered some interesting significance with Luffy, and interestingly, even Franky, and lastly, Imu. There are sure to be tons more numerological parallels, but this is just what I’ve leaned into so far!

text: Look at Gol D. Roger’s hat. He has a blue and a red feather in it. If you then look at Blackbeards hat, there are two feathers of the same type but both of them are red. We know Roger knows what happened during the Void Century and the truth about the world, even though we still don’t know any specifics at this point in time. Blue and Red. Vegapunk mentioned that there were once two ideologies that fought each other during the Void Century. The colors blue and red might be the representative colors for those ideologies. The Poneglyphs come in blue and red. There are two enourmous formations perpendicular to each other which are also blue and red that divide the world into its 4 oceans. The Grand Line and the Red Line. The Red Line, which is also where the Celestial Dragons reside, hinders you from free traversal while the Grand Line, where pirates are in search for the One Piece, is a call to adventure. Dorry and Brogy, the captains of the Giant Warrior Pirates bear the nicknames “Red and Blue Ogres”. What does Punk Hazard look like? Buggy and Shanks are currently Yonko, were on Roger’s ship and have blue and red hair. (While Blackbeard and Gear 5 Luffy are opposing forces too with their signature colors being black and white). Interestingly, the color pallete for Vivi, Shirahoshi, Momonosuke and Luffy is very similar to the Yonko color pallete. (Blue, pink as a shade of red, black and white). I also think that the aforementioned royals end up being the “pilots” for the Ancient Weapons, with Vivi being related to Uranus, the sky weapon, since she is heavily related to birds (Karoo, Pell, Big News Morgans). Could that mysterious giant egg on Roger’s ship be related to Uranus? We know that Shirahoshi controls the Sea Kings and Momo is able to command Zunesha. Kaido and Momonosuke were opposing forces during the Wano Kuni Arc and their dragon forms are blue and pink (which again could be considered a shade of red). There is also an anime-only scene in episode 1015 that I think a lot about. It’s the one where Yamato realises that Luffy and Roger share the same dream. In it, Luffy represented by the color blue and Roger represented by the color red, come together and become one. I am not saying I am 100% correct, but whatever ends up being true at the end… I am thoroughly convinced that you definitely should keep an eye on the colors blue and red since they seem to bear a significant amount of importance to Oda. I mean even the logo of the series features those two colors.
label: analysis

text: The Adam and Eve trees point towards the allegory of the garden of Eden (or what we know as the Paradise half of the One Piece World). A snake of some description, perhaps literally a snake this time (God of the Forest?), convinced humans to eat forbidden fruits (Devil Fruits) to gain knowledge and power. Presumably to fight against the winner of the war in the first world - the Nerona Imu and the Celestial Dragons. The roots of the Eve tree bring sunlight to the sea floor, an allegory for freedom and hope in the One Piece story. We do not know where the top of the Eve tree emerges in the One Piece world but I'm sure it's somewhere significant. The fact that they are called Devil Fruits implies that someone, at some point, did a 'deal with the devil' to make the wishes of people come true. I strongly believe this is when Imu rose to power. WWe've seen Gunko-Imu use occult powers of summoning which indicates this.
label: analysis

text: Now, to be clear my change is not drastic because my position was never drastic. My position was agnostic in favor of Shanks, but now I have switched to agnostic in favor of Mihawk. What remains constant is that there are reasons not to affirm one or the other as the stronger of the two. Why was I more inclined towards Shanks being stronger? Narrative. Luffy aspires to transcend Shanks, and Zoro aspires to transcend Mihawk, and because Luffy is stronger than Zoro, I was more inclined towards Shanks being stronger than Mihawk (without a 100% certainty). Mihawk's title didn't move me because I don't trust titles in One Piece. Zoro's dream didn't move me either because in order to become the strongest swordman, he just has to become stronger than whoever is the strongest swordsman. The position itself is dynamic, what would happen if S-Hawk keeps developing and becomes stronger than Mihawk? What then? Does that stop Zoro's dream? Only if he fails to trascend Mihawk 2.0 steel blades electric boogaloo. What changed? I zoomed into the nature of Luffy's dream and the nature of Zoro's dream. Luffy's dream is not to be the strongest; strength is merely a tool for reaching that dream, but with Zoro, strength is the goal itself. Shanks is, above all, a bold, charismatic leader and a protector of the weak, while Mihawk is a warrior. Luffy's main role is being a leader and liberator; Zoro's main role is being a warrior. So, in order for Shanks to be the walking milestone of Luffy, he doesn't need to be stronger than Mihawk; he only needs more leadership and charisma. In fact, Mihawk being stronger than Shanks would serve as a way to make emphasis on who Luffy is and who he aspires to be.
label: reaction
